pois.title: Centro Ciencia Viva do Algarve
pois.summary: This hands-on science museum is an excellent interactive experience for all the family.

pois.title: Chefe Branco
pois.summary: Just off the seafront in Faro, Chefe Branco specialises in producing great food, served by friendly staff at very reasonable prices.

pois.title: Columbus Bar
pois.summary: If you fancy a delicious cocktail before dinner, stop by the Columbus Bar at Praça Dom Francisco Gomes.

pois.title: Estoi
pois.summary: Just north of Faro you’ll find Estói, which is a great location for a day trip.

pois.title: Estádio Algarve
pois.summary: Built for the Euro 2004 tournament, the 30,000-capacity Estádio Algarve is the home of the Gibraltar national football team and hosts the final match of the international women’s football tournament, the Algarve Cup.

pois.title: Fabrica dos Sentidos
pois.summary: Fábrica dos Sentidos is a unique performance space near the waterfront in Faro.

pois.title: Faro e Benfica
pois.summary: Located at the mouth of the Marina, Faro e Benfica is favoured by locals for its fresh lobster and other fish dishes.

pois.title: Faro Jewish Heritage Centre
pois.summary: The Jewish Heritage Centre tells the story of the Faro Jews, who fled the inquisition at the end of the 14th century and settled in Gibraltar and North Africa.

pois.title: Faz Gostos
pois.summary: Located in the old town near the cathedral, Faz Gostos offers an elevated take on the rustic local cuisine.

pois.title: Galeria Trem
pois.summary: Just off the centre square of Faro’s old town in a former military barracks, you’ll find Galería de Arte Trem.

pois.title: Gardy Croissanteria
pois.summary: Gardy Patisserie on the Rua de Santo António is a lovely place to go to get your pastry fix.

pois.title: Hickey's Irish Pub
pois.summary: Hickey's Irish Pub is a popular favourite in Alvor, near Faro – and every town needs its Irish bar, right?

pois.title: Igreja da Misericórdia
pois.summary: Stunning blue ceramic tiles depicting the stations of the cross line the walls of Igreja da Misericórdia (meaning Compassion).

pois.title: Igreja de Nossa Senhora do Carmo
pois.summary: Igreja de Nossa Senhora do Carmo in Faro is as about as elaborate as churches come.

pois.title: Igreja de São Francisco
pois.summary: Dedicated to one of the most venerated religious figures in history, Saint Francis of Assisi, the Igreja de São Francisco in Faro attracts visitors in their thousands.

pois.title: Jardim da Alameda João de Deus
pois.summary: Come to the tranquil Jardim da Alameda João de Deus to see the largest landscaped garden in Faro.

pois.title: Kadoc
pois.summary: If you fancy partying with 8,000 other fun-loving revellers, get yourself down to Kadoc on Cerca da Areia, Boliqueime, about halfway between Albufeira and Vilamoura.

pois.title: Maritime Museum
pois.summary: The sea has played a huge role in the Algarve's past.

pois.title: Municipal Museum
pois.summary: A visit to the Municipal Museum will give you an insight into the town’s Roman and Moorish history, a great deal of which is in evidence today.

pois.title: Olhão
pois.summary: For those in search of the freshest seafood, a visit to the historic fishing port of Olhão, a short hop from Faro, is a must.

pois.title: Praia de Faro
pois.summary: Just a short cycle or bus ride from the city centre, you’ll find Praia de Faro, with its five kilometres of golden sands and brilliant blue sea.

pois.title: Ria Formosa National Park
pois.summary: If you want to get away from the pool to take in some stunning natural scenery and wildlife, head for Ria Formosa Natural Park.

pois.title: Se7e Pedras Restaurant & Wine Bar
pois.summary: Se7e Pedras Restaurant & Wine Bar, at Travessa dos Arcos 7, has a cosy, wine-cellar vibe; all raw-brick walls and low arches that you have to duck your head to get through.

pois.title: Taska
pois.summary: Specialising in European, Portuguese and Mediterranean food, with a slant towards fish and seafood, Taska, on Rua do Alportel in Faro, is a charming place that prides itself on making a real fuss of its diners.

pois.title: Church of São Lourenço
pois.summary: Inside the Igreja of São Lourenço, the walls are completely covered in the most dramatic azulejo painted ceramic tiles, which recount scenes from the life of São Lourenço, including his death in Rome.

pois.title: Ria Formosa
pois.summary: What better way to spend a winter break on the Algarve than with a spot of birdwatching?

pois.title: Capela dos Ossos
pois.summary: The Capela dos Ossos was built by a Franciscan monk in the 16th century and lined with the exhumed skeletons of hundreds of his peers as a meditation on mortality and eternity.

pois.title: Cathedral of Faro
pois.summary: Like many of Faro’s churches, its Cathedral was built on the site of a Moorish mosque.

pois.title: Christmas in Vila Real de Santo Antonio
pois.summary: Want to experience the Algarve at its most authentic?

pois.title: Day trip to Lisbon
pois.summary: Europe’s second oldest city is perfect if you want the best of both worlds – city break and beach holiday.

pois.title: Day trip to Seville
pois.summary: Stroll down the cobbled streets of the Santa Cruz quarter, making your way to the Alcazar Palace drinking in the scent of orange blossom, or climb the steps in the Giralda tower for the view across the city.

pois.title: Ilha da Culatra
pois.summary: Grab your beach parasol and head to Ilha da Culatra for the day.

pois.title: Loulé Carnival
pois.summary: If you want to explore a lesser-known side of the Algarve, Loulé is a great place to start.

pois.title: Mercado de Loulé
pois.summary: Discover Mercado de Loulé, an incredible covered market in this traditional town's heart.

pois.title: Mercado Municipal Market
pois.summary: An impressive building in the heart of town, the Mercado Municipal de Faro is a lovely, cool place to come for a wander and to pick up fresh, local produce and people-watch.

pois.title: Monchique Mountains
pois.summary: Potter along the narrow cobbled streets enjoying a honey-soaked cake from one of the many cake shops or take in the restorative spa waters at Caldas de Monchique.

pois.title: Praia de Vale do Lobo
pois.summary: Backed by red-tinged cliffs and carpeted in golden sand, Praia de Vale do Lobo is a real stunner.

pois.title: Praia do Ancao
pois.summary: Part of Ria Formosa Natural Park, Praia do Ancao is a long sandy beach backed by sand dunes and pine trees.

pois.title: Praia do Garrao
pois.summary: Swit swoo, Praia do Garrao is real beauty!

pois.title: Praia do Tonel
pois.summary: This sweep of golden sand feels the full force of the Atlantic Ocean, making it one of the region’s top surfing spots.

pois.title: Regional Museum of the Algarve
pois.summary: The Regional Museum of the Algarve takes you back in time to how life was lived in the area many years ago.

pois.title: Sagres
pois.summary: If you prefer a more secluded getaway, you’ll love the laid-back vibe of Sagres where you can take in the rugged landscape of Europe’s most south-westerly point.

pois.title: Sao Bras
pois.summary: Swing by the old-school village of São Brás to discover the Algarve at its most authentic.

pois.title: Seven Hanging Valleys Walking trail
pois.summary: Pinch-me panoramas are on the cards along this coastal cliff walk with its surf-splashed rock formations and secret coves.

pois.title: Silves
pois.summary: If you love old-world charm, you’ll love the former capital of the Algarve where you can lunch in cafés along the River Arade or take in the expansive views of the countryside from the red-bricked castle.

pois.title: Slide & Splash Waterpark
pois.summary: Water slides, spa treatments – yep, you'll find them all at Slide & Splash!

pois.title: Vale do Lobo Tennis Academy
pois.summary: One of the largest tennis facilities awaits in Vale do Lobo.
